name: Jimmy Giuffre
description: American jazz clarinet and saxophone player (1921–2008)
abstract: James Peter Giuffre ( JOO-fray, Italian: [dʒufˈfrɛ]; April 26, 1921 – April 24, 2008) was an American jazz clarinetist, saxophonist, composer, and arranger. He is known for developing forms of jazz which allowed for free interplay between the musicians, anticipating forms of free improvisation.

about_quotes.speaker: Janis Siegel
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy Guiffre was one who taught me, when I was arranging “Four Brothers” I was going through some of the voices with him
about_quotes.doc_title: Janis Siegel interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: It was Jimmy Giuffre. ... I was hired to do the history courses and composition and arranging, as was George Russell, also, arranging. ... Jimmy Giuffre, who was on the faculty ... And also, as you know, he wrote a bunch of Third Stream pieces, many of which I recorded.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Konitz Lee
about_quotes.evidence: The next record is a few months later in May of fifty-nine. Lee Konitz Meets Jimmy Giuffre and the Saxophone Section. ... Yeah that was a nice one.
about_quotes.doc_title: Konitz Lee — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Holman Bill
about_quotes.evidence: Shorty and I were doing that, Jimmy Giuffre, Remo Belli, Howard on bass – Howard Rumsey – and Frank Patchen on piano. It was a fun gig for me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Holman Bill — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bley Carla
about_quotes.evidence: certain musicians, the people that played my music and asked me for tunes were George Russell, Jimmy Giuffre...
about_quotes.doc_title: Bley Carla — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jim Hall
about_quotes.evidence: I think I went to Europe with Giuffre once... Probably it was an outgrowth of the trio stuff with Giuffre
about_quotes.doc_title: Jim Hall — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
